Natural gas and propane fuel hookup

faqs.question: How long does a generator installation take?
faqs.answer: A typical standby generator installation is completed in one to two days once permits are in hand. Day one covers the pad, generator set, fuel line, and transfer switch; final electrical tie-in and the startup commissioning follow. Larger whole-house systems or installs requiring a gas meter upgrade can take longer, and we provide a clear schedule before work begins.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to install a standby generator in Monmouth County?
faqs.answer: Yes. Standby generator installations in New Jersey require both electrical and, where a gas line is involved, plumbing/mechanical permits through your local municipality, plus an inspection. We coordinate the permit process and schedule the required inspections as part of the installation.

faqs.question: What size generator do I need for my home?
faqs.answer: Generator sizing depends on the square footage of your home, whether you want to power essential circuits only or the whole house, and large loads like central air, well pumps, and electric ranges. We perform an on-site load calculation rather than guessing, so the unit is sized correctly for your actual usage.

faqs.question: Should I run my generator on natural gas or propane?
faqs.answer: If your home already has natural gas service from the utility, that is usually the simplest and lowest-maintenance fuel because it never needs refilling. Homes without gas service typically run on a propane tank. We assess what is available at your property and recommend the right fuel setup for your location.

name: Standby Generators
metaTitle: Standby Generators in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Automatic standby generators for {location}, NJ homes. Whole-house backup power that starts on its own during JCP&L outages. Site survey and sizing included.
description: Automatic standby generators that detect an outage and restore power to your home within seconds, with no manual setup.

faqs.question: How is a standby generator different from a portable generator?
faqs.answer: A standby generator is permanently installed outside your home, connected to a fixed fuel supply, and starts automatically the moment the power goes out — no cords, no gasoline, no manual setup. A portable generator must be rolled out, fueled, and connected by hand, and cannot safely power your home's wiring without additional equipment.

faqs.question: How quickly does a standby generator restore power?
faqs.answer: Most automatic standby systems detect an outage and transfer your home to generator power within seconds. You may notice the lights blink once as the automatic transfer switch hands off, then power is restored and stays on until utility service returns.

faqs.question: Will a standby generator run my whole house?
faqs.answer: It can, depending on the size of the unit and your home's electrical demand. Some homeowners choose a whole-house system that powers everything; others select an essential-circuit setup that covers heat, refrigeration, well pump, and key outlets at a lower cost. We size the system to your priorities during the on-site survey.

name: Generator Maintenance
metaTitle: Generator Maintenance in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Scheduled standby generator maintenance in {location}, NJ. Oil and filter changes, battery checks, and full system testing so your generator starts when you need it.
description: Scheduled maintenance that keeps your standby generator ready to start, including oil changes, filter service, and full system checks.

faqs.question: How often should a standby generator be serviced?
faqs.answer: Most standby generators should be serviced at least once a year, with an oil change typically due every year or after a set number of run hours, whichever comes first. Homes that experience frequent or long outages may need service more often because the unit accumulates run time. We track service intervals for the units we maintain.

faqs.question: What happens if I skip generator maintenance?
faqs.answer: A generator that sits unmaintained is the one most likely to fail when an outage actually hits. Old oil, a weak battery, fouled spark plugs, or stale fuel are the most common reasons a standby unit won't start. Regular maintenance catches these issues during good weather rather than during a storm.

faqs.question: Do you service generators you didn't install?
faqs.answer: Yes. We service and maintain existing standby generators regardless of who installed them. We start with a full inspection to document the unit's condition, then perform the maintenance it's due for and set you up on a regular service schedule.

name: Generator Repair
metaTitle: Generator Repair in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Standby generator repair and troubleshooting in {location}, NJ. Diagnosis of no-start, error codes, transfer switch faults, and electrical issues. Get it running again.
description: Diagnosis and repair of standby generators that won't start, throw error codes, or fail to transfer power during an outage.

faqs.question: Why won't my standby generator start?
faqs.answer: The most common causes of a no-start are a dead or weak battery, stale fuel, a tripped breaker, a fouled spark plug, or a control setting left in the off position after a previous service. We diagnose the root cause rather than swapping parts, then repair the specific failure and verify the unit starts and transfers correctly.

faqs.question: My generator runs but the house has no power — what's wrong?
faqs.answer: If the generator runs but power doesn't reach the home, the issue is usually in the automatic transfer switch or the electrical connection rather than the generator itself. This requires diagnosis of the switch, the wiring, and the controls. We test the full transfer path to isolate and repair the fault.

faqs.question: Do you offer emergency generator repair?
faqs.answer: We prioritize generator repairs during and after major outages when standby power matters most. Availability depends on storm conditions and demand. Keeping your unit on a regular maintenance schedule is the best way to avoid an emergency in the first place, since most failures are preventable.

name: Electrical Services
metaTitle: Electrical Services in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Generator-related electrical services in {location}, NJ. Transfer switches, panel upgrades, dedicated circuits, and code-compliant wiring for standby power systems.
description: Electrical work supporting generator and backup-power systems, including transfer switches, panel upgrades, and dedicated circuits.

faqs.question: What is a transfer switch and do I need one?
faqs.answer: A transfer switch is the device that safely moves your home between utility power and generator power. It is required for any permanently connected generator because it prevents dangerous backfeed onto the utility lines and protects your equipment. An automatic transfer switch does this on its own; a manual switch requires you to operate it during an outage.

faqs.question: Will my electrical panel need to be upgraded for a generator?
faqs.answer: Sometimes. Older or fully loaded panels may not have the capacity or space to safely add a generator circuit and transfer switch. We evaluate your existing panel during the assessment and recommend an upgrade only if it's genuinely required for a safe, code-compliant installation.

faqs.question: Are your electrical services code-compliant in New Jersey?
faqs.answer: All electrical work is performed to the National Electrical Code and New Jersey requirements, with permits and inspections where the work calls for them. Code-compliant installation is what keeps your system safe, insurable, and reliable over the long term.

name: Seasonal Oil Changes
metaTitle: Generator Oil Changes in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Seasonal generator oil and filter changes in {location}, NJ. Keep your standby unit ready for storm season with fresh oil, a clean filter, and a quick health check.
description: Seasonal oil and filter changes that keep your standby generator ready for storm and winter season.
features: Correct oil grade for the season

faqs.question: How often does a generator need an oil change?
faqs.answer: Most standby generators need an oil change once a year or after a set number of run hours, whichever comes first. Units that run frequently during outages accumulate hours faster and may need more frequent changes. A seasonal change before storm season is a reliable rule of thumb for most homeowners.

faqs.question: Why does the season matter for generator oil?
faqs.answer: Oil viscosity changes with temperature, and the correct grade helps the engine start and run cleanly in cold New Jersey winters versus warm summers. Using the right seasonal oil reduces wear and improves cold-start reliability when you need the generator most.

faqs.question: Can I change the generator oil myself?
faqs.answer: Some homeowners do, but the oil type, capacity, and filter vary by model, and an incorrect oil or an overfill can cause problems. Having it done as part of a service visit also means the battery, connections, and start function get checked at the same time.

name: Battery Replacement
metaTitle: Generator Battery Replacement in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Standby generator battery replacement in {location}, NJ. A weak battery is the #1 reason generators fail to start. Fast testing and replacement before storm season.
description: Testing and replacement of standby generator batteries, the most common reason a unit fails to start during an outage.
features: Correct replacement battery for your model
Proper disposal of the old battery

faqs.question: How long does a generator battery last?
faqs.answer: A standby generator battery typically lasts a few years, but cold winters, heat, and infrequent use shorten that lifespan. Because a weak battery is the single most common reason a generator won't start, many homeowners replace it on a regular schedule rather than waiting for it to fail.

faqs.question: How do I know if my generator battery is failing?
faqs.answer: Warning signs include slow cranking during the self-test, a battery warning on the controller, or the unit failing to start. The most reliable way to know is a load test, which measures whether the battery can actually deliver starting power rather than just showing a resting voltage.

faqs.question: Will replacing the battery fix a generator that won't start?
faqs.answer: Often, yes — a dead battery is the most frequent no-start cause. But if the battery tests fine, the problem lies elsewhere, such as fuel, ignition, or the controller. We test before replacing so you only pay to fix the actual issue.

name: Generator Inspections
metaTitle: Generator Inspections in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Standby generator inspections in {location}, NJ. Full condition assessment of an existing or newly purchased home's generator, with a written report of findings.
description: Comprehensive inspection of an existing standby generator's condition, ideal for new homeowners or pre-storm peace of mind.

faqs.question: Why would I need a generator inspection?
faqs.answer: An inspection is valuable when you buy a home with an existing generator and don't know its service history, before storm season, or when a unit hasn't run in a while. It documents the generator's true condition so you know whether it's ready to perform or needs attention before the next outage.

faqs.question: What does a generator inspection include?
faqs.answer: We check the engine, battery, fuel system, oil, filters, and the automatic transfer switch, then run the unit and verify it transfers power correctly. You receive a written report describing the condition of each component and any recommended repairs or maintenance.

faqs.question: I just bought a house with a generator — should I inspect it?
faqs.answer: Yes. Without the prior owner's service records, there's no way to know when the generator was last maintained or whether it still works. An inspection gives you a clear baseline and a maintenance plan so you can rely on the unit during the first storm you face.

name: Load Testing
metaTitle: Generator Load Testing in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Standby generator load testing in {location}, NJ. Verify your generator can actually carry your home's electrical load under real demand, not just idle in a self-test.
description: Load testing that confirms your generator can carry your home's real electrical demand, not just run unloaded during a self-test.
features: Identification of weak or failing components

faqs.question: What is generator load testing?
faqs.answer: Load testing applies a real electrical load to your generator and measures whether it can produce and sustain the required power. A generator's weekly self-test usually runs unloaded, so it can mask problems that only appear under demand. Load testing reveals whether the unit will actually carry your home during an outage.

faqs.question: Why isn't the weekly self-test enough?
faqs.answer: The self-test confirms the engine starts and runs, but it typically doesn't apply your home's full electrical load. A unit can pass its self-test and still struggle or fail when an outage forces it to power your air conditioning, well pump, and appliances at once. Load testing closes that gap.

faqs.question: How often should a generator be load tested?
faqs.answer: Load testing is commonly done annually, often alongside routine maintenance, and any time you suspect the generator isn't performing as it should. It's also worthwhile after major repairs to confirm the unit is back to full capacity.

name: Preventive Maintenance
metaTitle: Generator Preventive Maintenance Plans in {location} NJ | Monmouth County Generac
metaDescription: Preventive maintenance plans for standby generators in {location}, NJ. Scheduled service that keeps your unit reliable and catches problems before the next outage.
description: Ongoing preventive maintenance plans that keep your standby generator reliable through scheduled, tracked service visits.

faqs.question: What is a preventive maintenance plan?
faqs.answer: A preventive maintenance plan puts your standby generator on a recurring service schedule so oil changes, battery checks, and system tests happen on time without you having to track them. The goal is to catch wear and weak components during good weather so the unit is ready when an outage hits.

faqs.question: Is a maintenance plan worth it for a home generator?
faqs.answer: For most homeowners, yes. A standby generator only earns its value if it starts during an outage, and the failures that prevent that — dead batteries, old oil, stale fuel — are exactly what scheduled maintenance prevents. A plan also means the work is tracked and you're not relying on memory.

faqs.question: What's included in your preventive maintenance plan?
faqs.answer: Plans include scheduled visits with oil and filter service, battery testing, a full system inspection, and a transfer-switch and run test, along with service-history tracking and reminders. We'll recommend an annual or semi-annual schedule based on how often your generator runs.
